response: interfaces offer flexibility that abstract classes cannot match because they allow unrelated classes to share behavior without forcing a rigid inheritance hierarchy. when architects design systems they often anticipate future changes or extensions that are impossible to predict fully at the start. relying solely on abstract classes creates a lack of flexibility since java only supports single inheritance. by programming to an interface you can mix and match capabilities across different class trees much like adding ingredients to a salad. this approach prevents tight coupling where a file might need to be processed by many different types of objects that do not share a common parent. the path to a scalable architecture often involves defining contracts via interfaces rather than assuming fixed relationships. this strategy ensures that even if the initial design seems incomplete the system can evolve without breaking existing code.
